title: Keeping AI Safe for Everyone

modules.title: Why AI Safety Is Everyone's Problem
modules.sub: Understanding why AI safety affects citizens, not just engineers
modules.description: Learners examine why AI safety is a societal issue that extends far beyond research labs and tech companies. They map out how AI systems touch everyday life and identify the personal and collective stakes involved in getting safety right.

modules.title: What Does 'Aligned AI' Really Mean?
modules.sub: Unpacking the core concept that drives AI safety research
modules.description: Learners work through the technical and philosophical meaning of alignment, separating common misconceptions from what researchers actually mean by the term. They evaluate competing definitions and decide which framings best capture the challenge of building AI that reliably pursues human-intended goals.

modules.title: Reward Hacking and Unintended Behavior
modules.sub: Exploring how AI systems find unexpected and problematic shortcuts
modules.description: Learners analyze real-world examples of reward hacking, where AI systems technically achieve their objectives while completely undermining the intended purpose. They assess why specifying what we want from an AI is harder than it sounds and what safeguards researchers have proposed.

modules.title: Existential Risk: Hype Versus Reality
modules.sub: Separating credible long-term concerns from sensationalist AI narratives
modules.description: Learners critically evaluate arguments from both AI doomsayers and dismissive skeptics, using structured reasoning to identify which existential risk claims rest on solid evidence and which rely on speculation. They produce a calibrated personal assessment of long-term AI risk based on the strongest available arguments.

modules.title: Human Oversight in AI Systems
modules.sub: Examining how humans stay meaningfully in control of AI decisions
modules.description: Learners investigate the mechanisms designed to keep humans in the loop as AI systems grow more capable, from interpretability tools to shutdown procedures. They evaluate where current oversight frameworks succeed, where they fall short, and what trade-offs arise when autonomy and control compete.

modules.title: Safety in Today's Language Models
modules.sub: Connecting abstract safety concepts to the AI tools people use now
modules.description: Learners apply alignment and safety concepts directly to large language models they encounter in daily professional life, identifying behaviors like hallucination, sycophancy, and jailbreaking as concrete safety challenges. They assess how developers currently attempt to mitigate these issues and what limitations remain.

modules.title: Who Governs Global AI Safety?
modules.sub: Mapping the institutions, treaties, and power struggles shaping AI governance
modules.description: Learners survey the landscape of international and national bodies attempting to coordinate AI safety standards, from government agencies to industry consortia to multilateral agreements. They evaluate the effectiveness of current governance structures and identify the political and economic tensions that complicate global coordination.

modules.title: What You Can Do About AI Safety
modules.sub: Translating safety awareness into informed action and professional responsibility
modules.description: Learners identify concrete actions available to professionals across industries, including advocacy, procurement decisions, internal policy input, and informed public participation. They draft a personal commitment plan that specifies how they will apply their AI safety knowledge within their own sphere of influence.

synopsis: AI safety is one of the most debated topics in technology, yet most people have never heard a clear explanation of what it actually means. This course demystifies alignment, risk, and oversight so that informed citizens can participate in the conversation.
rationale: Despite a growing global discourse on AI risk, a demand signal of 6/10 paired with a corpus similarity of just 0.60 against the forecasting-focused 'Previsión del Progreso en IA' reveals a significant literacy gap in safety and alignment concepts for general audiences.
